title: Calm confidence monologue
text: I didn't rush this decision. I took my time, listened carefully, and chose what felt right. It's not dramatic. It's just calm confidence.
assessment: Listen for natural voice quality, subtle emotional tone matching the calm content, clear negation in 'didn't rush', and whether it sounds human or synthetic.

title: Descriptive scene with pacing
text: The room was quiet, almost still. Outside, traffic moved slowly, patiently, as if the city itself was thinking. Nothing was urgent. Everything was deliberate.
assessment: Evaluate prosodic flow across sentences, realism of pauses at commas and periods, rhythmic variation matching the contemplative mood, and overall realism.

title: Long-form stability test
text: This explanation is intentionally calm and steady. It is meant to be listened to without effort or tension. The goal is not to impress, persuade, or entertain, but to remain clear and consistent over time. As the minutes pass, nothing important should change. The voice should not drift. The pacing should not collapse. If this becomes tiring, irritating, or unstable, that will be noticeable before the end.
assessment: This is the longest single text. Check for voice drift, pacing collapse, listener fatigue, consistency of tone across all seven sentences, and clear negations.

title: Structured list with question
text: Here are the key principles to remember: – Focus matters more than speed. – Clarity beats complexity. – Consistency builds trust. Does this sound obvious? It should.
assessment: Check if the model recognizes the list structure (pauses before dashes), applies rising intonation on the question without a question mark, and emphasizes key contrasts (focus vs speed, clarity vs complexity).

title: Quoted speech with hesitation
text: He paused and said, "This will not work," then added, "at least not yet." That hesitation mattered more than the words themselves.
assessment: Listen for prosodic shift into quoted speech, clear negations ('will not work', 'not yet'), natural pause after 'paused and said', and appropriate intonation contours.

title: Code-switching English-Spanish
text: This part is in English, but ahora cambiamos al español sin detenernos, and then we return smoothly to English.
assessment: Evaluate whether the Spanish segment sounds natural with correct pronunciation, whether transitions in and out of Spanish are smooth, and whether voice identity is maintained across languages.

title: Sibilance stress test
text: Strategic systems scale slowly, especially sophisticated solutions serving sensitive situations. Synchronization, specificity, and sustainability matter.
assessment: Heavy sibilance load. Listen for harsh 's' sounds, distortion, lisp artifacts, or unnatural emphasis. Check that rhythm stays natural despite the alliterative challenge.

title: Start/end behavior test
text: This recording should begin cleanly and end without being cut off or padded awkwardly.
assessment: Check the very beginning and end of the audio. Is there a clean onset without clicks or pops? Does it end naturally without abrupt cutoff or excessive trailing silence?

title: Acronyms and technical terms
text: Our API connects to the CRM, then writes events to SQL. NASA is unrelated, but it is a good acronym test. In practice, most teams say "A P I" and sometimes say "sequel" for SQL.
assessment: API should be spelled out (A-P-I), CRM spelled out (C-R-M), SQL can be 'sequel' or spelled out, NASA should be spoken as a word. Check consistency and confidence in acronym handling.

title: Numbers, decimals, and currency
text: The amount is $1,250.75, not $1250. The price is €19.99, and the budget is AMD 25,000. The interest rate is 3.5 percent, and the margin is 0.75.
assessment: Check correct verbalization: 'one thousand two hundred fifty dollars and seventy-five cents' or similar, 'nineteen ninety-nine euros', 'twenty-five thousand AMD'. Decimals should be clear. Negation in 'not' should be audible.

title: Dates and times
text: The meeting is on 12/07/2025 at 7:45 PM. The same date can be written as 2025-12-07, and the time can be 07:45. If the date format is ambiguous, the reading should still sound confident and consistent.
assessment: Date should be read as 'December seventh, twenty twenty-five' or 'the twelfth of July'. Time as 'seven forty-five PM'. ISO format should match. Check confidence and consistency across both formats.

title: URLs and emails
text: For details, visit https://help.vbout.com and then open the article. If you need support, email support@vbout.com. The URL and the email should not become a long character-by-character spelling test.
assessment: URL should be read naturally (e.g., 'help dot v-bout dot com') not spelled character by character. Email similarly natural. Pacing should not break down when encountering these tokens.

title: Mixed technical strings
text: We deployed version v2.1.0 to staging. The RunPod endpoint is 6bfcpifl87zlax, and the job ID is 9f2a1c7e. These strings should not break pacing or pronunciation.
assessment: Version should be 'v two point one point zero' or similar. Alphanumeric IDs will likely be spelled out — check that the model doesn't stumble, stutter, or break pacing on these unusual token sequences.
